Ah, the classic "bakit wala si Sara?" question — and honestly, it's the right question to ask, kasi ang daming tao akala nila co-perpetrator siya automatically just because she's a Duterte and former Davao mayor. But here's the real tea:
Si Sara, nasa waiting room pa lang, hindi pa siya sa main event.
The ICC didn't exclude her because she's innocent or protected — they excluded her from this specific charging document because that's how strategic prosecution works. Think of it like building a case laban sa drug cartel: you don't go after everyone agad-agad. You start with the kingpin (Rodrigo) and his clear command structure (PNP chiefs, DOJ officials, the people with actual paper trails showing direct orders).
Sara's situation is more nuanced — she's what they call a "secondary respondent," which sounds softer pero in reality, it just means "later, but not forgotten."
Here's why that matters:
1. Documentary evidence is king sa ICC. The current DCC focuses on the national drug war machinery — yung may clear chain of command from Malacañang down to the tokhang operations. Sara's alleged involvement is more localized (Davao-specific), and yung continuity ng Davao Death Squad template under her watch requires a different evidentiary package. Hindi pwedeng sabay lang i-lump in kung hindi pa solid yung build-up.
2. The ICC plays chess, not checkers. They don't blow their entire load sa first charge sheet. They secure the main targets muna, then expand outward. Sara being "on the horizon" doesn't mean she's safe — it means the prosecution is pacing itself. Kumbaga, si Duterte ang unang barko na babagsak; si Sara, sumunod na alon yan.
3. Her own statements are already being used against her family. The ICC literally cited Sara's public remarks (plus Baste's and Rodrigo's) to reject Duterte Sr.'s interim release plea. So she's already materially involved in the case — just not yet as a named co-perpetrator. That's a very thin line, and it can shift anytime new evidence surfaces or the prosecution decides to file amended charges.
4. "Secondary respondent" is legalese for "you're next in line." Walang ganap na safe space dito. The moment the ICC establishes the Davao template as the blueprint for the national drug war, Sara's role as Davao mayor during key years becomes extremely relevant. And unlike her father, she doesn't have the luxury of claiming she was "just following orders" — she was the local executive, the one allegedly continuing the very system that the ICC is prosecuting.
So bakit importante 'to?
Because Filipinos love false binaries: either you're charged or you're cleared. But international criminal justice doesn't work that way. Sara not being on the initial list doesn't mean vindication — it means strategic sequencing. The ICC is building a case that can actually stick, not just throwing spaghetti at the wall.
And here's the kicker: she herself said she believes she's on the ICC's radar. That's not paranoia — that's someone who knows exactly what kind of receipts exist. Kaya nga walang kampante dito. The fact that she's publicly acknowledging it tells you she's preparing for the possibility, which is very different from the posture of someone who genuinely believes they're in the clear.
Bottom line: Sara's absence from this list is procedural, not substantive. The ICC is building a ladder, and right now, they're focused on the top rungs — yung mga direktang nag-design at nag-execute ng drug war machinery. But make no mistake: yung buong ladder, tinitingnan nila. Sara is still part of the structure, still part of the political and operational continuity, and still very much within the scope of accountability.
Kaya ang tanong hindi dapat "bakit wala siya?" — ang tanong dapat ay "kailan siya?" Because in cases like this, it's rarely a matter of if, but when. #LakaFuerte

Oo nga, "justice delayed" daw—pero ang totoo, justice wasn't just delayed, pinatulog muna kasi puyat tayong lahat sa gaslighting.
What hits different about this ICC development is how it dismantles the very defense mechanism that kept this machinery oiled for years: yung narrative na "one bad apple," "rogue cops," or "misunderstood intentions." The Document Containing the Charges doesn't just point fingers—it connects the dots from Davao to Malacañang, from street-level executions to Cabinet-level complicity. It names names. And suddenly, "sumunod lang" transforms from alibi to admission of guilt.
The genius—if you can call it that—of Duterte's war on drugs was that it weaponized our collective exhaustion with crime and dressed up mass murder as decisive governance. Kaya nga maraming nagsabi, "eh at least may ginagawa." But the ICC is now doing what our own institutions couldn't or wouldn't: treating the system as the crime scene, not just the corpses.
Ang ironic: the same people who screamed "sovereignty" when we withdrew from the ICC are now facing the reality that international law doesn't care about your press releases. You can't "unsubscribe" from crimes against humanity like it's a newsletter. The court's jurisdiction locked in the moment the alleged crimes began, and no amount of political theater changes that.
But here's what terrifies me: even with all this—ICC warrants, named co-perpetrators, a confirmation hearing on the calendar—there are still those who will frame this as "destabilization," as "NPA propaganda," as anything except accountability for thousands of extrajudicial killings. Kasi once you admit the drug war was murder dressed as policy, you have to reckon with the fact that we let it happen. Some of us even cheered it on.
So yes, justice may be slow. Pero ang mas masakit? Justice is also a mirror. And not everyone is ready to look. #LakaFuerte

105.text: FAKE SURVEY🤑🤮🤮🤮

A survey is a snapshot, not a command. Our votes should be based on values, records, and issues—not on numbers released at strategic moments.

In an environment saturated with noise, this kind of voter education matters. An informed public is still the strongest safeguard of a functioning democracy.

This survey is also a form of marketing (campaigning) to target those who are apolitical, centrist and those at the fringe who’ve been growing doubtful of the politician they currently support. It reinforces the perception that whoever is at the top will eventually win so what is the use of voting other candidates? It also gives false impression that the top candidate must have a good reputation to earn such popularity.

I will always remember how Bam A. and Kiko P. defied all survey results during the recent senatorial elections. The sampling is too small for a nation as diverse (divided?) as ours. I doubt it if there’s real representation in that sample.

I don’t know. I’ve never trusted surveys. As someone who entertains one particular conspiracy theory—that the last few elections since Duterte became President have been rigged—I’ve always felt that the surveys were support mechanisms to give election results more credibility. 

But who knows really? Maybe the cheats stopped cheating because they couldn’t, because elections are now automated. And maybe the Comelec isn’t the king maker we perceive them to be. Maybe they’re truly just arbiters.

But, okay. It may not be fair to suspect surveying companies to be paid propagandists. But, no matter the intention, they do serve as campaign tools. And, for a big part of our history, the supposed antagonists always come out shining. But if the survey results didn’t reflect the general sentiment of the voter—that their voting patterns aren’t affected by issues—then more people would question their accuracy.

Whether we have surveys, or not, many of us know that a big portion of the electorate do not see the direct impact of corruption to the state of their (mis)fortunes. Many can’t imagine what a better life could be like. So, how can you regret something if you didn’t even realize it was bad? That it could’ve been much better? And I know this to be true. Many of our countrymen and women submit to the “destiny” angle. It’s far simpler and easier than entertaining the what ifs. 

Trying to get them to stop voting for the corrupt, by explaining clearly the impact of corruption, isn’t enough. It’s short term, and may easily be forgotten as soon as the ayuda starts coming in. But we have to try. Because we can’t wait for the next generation to fix our problems. It might be too late by then. And how can we even hope the next generation will fix things, if the system which will provide their formation is still being ran by the same corrupt people in government?

So, no choice: we, who know how corruption must be nipped in the bud, have to try to help enlighten those who still don’t see how this is do or die. It is a gargantuan task. But who else will do it? Not those who stand to benefit from ignorance. 

This is why I now feel that Leni should try again. She had the right mechanism the last time. And this isn’t the same as trying the same method—which lost—to hope for a win. It’s a continuance of what had already began; extending the coverage of its reach. 

A big part of the campaign involved house-to-house, and dialogue. The method of engagement was carefully curated, and I saw how effective it was. At the moment, I felt confident that we had imparted enough food for thought. And that’s a bit more than none at all. And if the voter won’t even consider the possibilities of how voting for someone who isn’t corrupt will improve their lives, then we’re doomed.

It’s easy to expect deep analysis from every voter, but the truth is many Filipinos are carrying the heavy demands of daily survival and caring for their families. For them, survey results may be the only political information they have the time or space to absorb — not out of indifference, but out of necessity.

Yes, we should say thank you to the framers of the 1987 Constitution.
They restored constitutional rule after dictatorship and gave the country an orderly framework for governance. That achievement is real and enduring.

But gratitude must not silence honest judgment.

A Constitution is not a commemorative document—it is an operating system for power. And in that role, the 1987 Constitution fell short in one critical respect: it left too much room for ambiguity.

Every line, every paragraph, and every mechanism—especially those involving accountability, impeachment, separation of powers, and limits on authority—should have been expressed with precision so clear that no framer, no justice, no elder would be needed decades later to explain what it “really” means.

Ambiguity is not a harmless gap. In a low-trust political environment like ours, ambiguity becomes a weapon.

It allows:
the legislature to twist procedures to protect allies or target enemies,
the executive to exploit silence and gray areas to expand power,
the judiciary to reinterpret settled rules and effectively rewrite constitutional processes,
and the public to lose faith as outcomes appear arbitrary, political, or inconsistent.

What we are witnessing today—confusion over impeachment, conflicting interpretations among the branches, and citizens forced to rely on retired framers and justices to decode the Constitution—is not a sign of constitutional maturity. It is a sign of structural weakness.

A Constitution should speak for itself.
It should not require press conferences, televised explanations, or appeals to the intent of men now in their seventies and eighties just to function.

Yes, the framers acted in good faith.
Yes, they faced extraordinary historical circumstances.
But good faith is not enough to restrain bad faith in the future.

By leaving crucial provisions open-ended—trusting institutions and political actors to “do the right thing”—the Constitution underestimated how power behaves when left room to maneuver. That miscalculation has contributed directly to the institutional friction, paralysis, and legitimacy crises we see today across all three branches of government, and in the minds of the people themselves.

So we thank the framers—not because their work was perfect, but because it was a beginning.

And precisely because we honor that beginning, we must say this clearly:

A Constitution that needs its framers to explain it is a Constitution that did not go far enough. 
And a Constitution that tolerates ambiguity at the highest levels of power invites the very chaos it was meant to prevent.

What we need is an honest-to-goodness, real and truthful Anti-Political dynasty law.
To some, dynasties it may be advantageous to constituents at some degrees, on the issues of accountability & transparency, although very seldom. But mostly not.

One that will bar and disqualify family members, up to the fourth degree of sanguinity and affinity, to vie for different positions, at any levels, all at the same time and terms.

OEC amendments suggestions:
> Do not allow multiple family members up to the 4th civil degree of consanguinity & affinity to run for multiple positions, at all levels, at the same term. This would allow to level the playing fields.
> Scrap the mandate of Comelec to decide and disqualify who's nuisance or not. Prolonging the name list in ballots would just cost the gov't meager costs, compared to billions lost to corruptions.
> Do not allow candidates who are presently facing cases in courts, whether civil or criminal, until cleared, acquitted or dismissed, with finality. This would avoid the dilemma of having an elected official and convicted later on and encarcerated, then having a seating elected official working behind bars, while an appeal is in progress.
> Elected officials who have reached and finished the maximum term limits should not be allowed to vie for a lower position anymore, in the next elections, especially right after the expiration of his term. Should one wishes to vie for a position, it should be a higher one from the recent.
Ex:
✓ When a Brgy chairman reached his term limit, he should not be allowed to run for Brgy Kgd anymore. If he wishes, he should vie for a city/municipal position.
This goes for other positions in the higher level, the city/municipal, district, region and national.
Once one reaches the highest post, he should not be allowed anymore to run for any lower position. When a president finished his term, he should not be allowed to run for any position anymore in the lower level. This would break the cycle. Take the case of Erap and Gma, it sucks.
> We should revert back to the 2-party system from the present Multi-Party system. This would strengthen the political party system, strengthen the principle based party system to avoid the umbrella system, the merging of political parties just for the election season convenience. Once elected, diffuse, say goodbye because of different principles and positions on issues.
With the Multi-Party system, personal vested interests are common grounds rather than principle-based stands.
> The present Partylist system should be revisited, amended and stick to the original purpose of its adaptation, if not scrapping it totally. It has been the escape route of most political clans and used as fallbacks after reaching term limits.
Presently, many of them DO NOT, as in, truthfully represent the so-called marginalized, underrepresented sectors of society. In actual practice, they are only being used as political convenience, political scapegoats.
> Give the Comelec moto propio powers to investigate, without the benefits of complaints or petitions for disqualification.
Basically, elections are being held every three years at the minimum. Except for special elections held for specific purposes. Speedy investigations should be prioritized. Most of the times resolutions are rendered when terms have already expired.

But, I’m wondering about the effects of a trade embargo, and if it would mean catastrophe for us. Certainly, when it comes to food, it would hurt us. Because we aren’t in a position to feed ourselves. But, for everything else, can we do without them?

I’m wondering about this because we seem to have an addiction to made in China products which, many of us, don’t want to have. There are actually a few movements to boycott buying of their products. And our experiences tell us that, even if they’re cheap, they’re also inferior in quality. A lot of them are. And, yet, it’s hard to resist. We keep falling into the trap. It’s like an addiction. Hard to resist. But, more often than not, there is regret later. So maybe going cold turkey wont be such a bad thing. 

It will surely hurt them to lose 1.4% of their annual sales. Specially since sales in the ASEAN has been a cushion for them from the decline in sales in the US. That’s still a $52B loss, and no matter how we put it, that’s huge.

But the whole point is the pagbabastos nila in our house. And, yes, world diplomacy is a little more complex than that. But no bully ever stopped bullying when the bullied just took everything. 

We know they won’t change. Maybe it’s time we start getting used to a world without any of their products…until they learn to behave. But, yeah, the government will have to work really hard in addressing the hit. They’ll actually have to do some real work. 

I apologize if this narrative doesn’t offer any solutions. Wish I had some. But maybe, imagining not having to cross your fingers that the product you bought isn’t a lemon, isn’t too bad either. And screw that ambassador.
